questions.question: Explain the file concept in operating systems and list any five file attributes.
keyPoints: File is a named collection of related information on secondary storage
Provides logical view of data storage
Hides physical storage details from users
Attributes: Name, Type, Size, Location, Protection, Timestamps, Identifier

questions.question: Differentiate between sequential access and random (direct) access methods for files.
keyPoints: Sequential: Records accessed one after another from beginning
Random: Can jump directly to any record position
Sequential uses file pointer that moves sequentially
Random uses seek() to move pointer to specific offset
Random access is faster for database-style operations

questions.question: What is file buffering? Explain its significance in file I/O operations.
keyPoints: Buffering uses memory to temporarily hold data during I/O
Reduces number of physical disk accesses
Improves performance by grouping operations
System maintains buffers in main memory
Data is transferred between buffer and disk in blocks

questions.question: Describe five basic file operations performed by operating systems.
keyPoints: Create: Make new empty file
Open: Prepare file for access, get file descriptor
Read: Retrieve data from file
Write: Store data to file
Seek: Move file pointer to specific position
Close: Terminate file access, free resources

questions.question: What is a file descriptor? How is it used in file operations?
keyPoints: File descriptor is a non-negative integer
Returned by open() system call
Used to identify open file in subsequent operations
Each process has its own file descriptor table
Operations like read(), write(), close() use file descriptor

questions.question: Explain the purpose of file locking in operating systems.
keyPoints: Prevents concurrent access conflicts
Ensures data consistency when multiple processes access file
Two types: shared lock (read) and exclusive lock (write)
Prevents race conditions and data corruption
Implemented through system calls like flock(), fcntl()

questions.question: What is the difference between internal and external fragmentation in file systems?
keyPoints: Internal: Wasted space within allocated blocks due to fixed sizes
External: Free scattered holes between allocated file blocks
Internal occurs in fixed allocation methods
External occurs in variable allocation methods
Both reduce effective storage utilization

questions.question: Explain the file system structure and discuss how file operations work. Include discussion on open file table and file control blocks.
keyPoints: File system provides hierarchical organization of files in directories
Directory contains file name to metadata mapping
File control block (FCB) stores all file attributes
Open file table maintains entries for currently open files
Each process has file descriptor table pointing to open file table
File operations: create, open, read, write, seek, close
System maintains file pointer for current position
Buffers used to improve I/O performance

questions.question: Discuss in detail the various file access methods supported by operating systems. Explain with examples where each method is appropriate.
keyPoints: Sequential access: read/write records in order, simple but slow for random access
Direct/Random access: jump to any position using seek, good for databases
Indexed access: uses index blocks for faster lookup of specific records
Relative access: uses relative record numbers from current position
Examples: Sequential for log files, tape backups; Random for databases, spreadsheets; Indexed for large database files
Discuss trade-offs: simplicity vs speed, storage overhead

questions.question: Explain what happens when a file is opened in an operating system. Describe the data structures involved and the sequence of operations.
keyPoints: User process calls open() with filename and mode
OS searches directory to locate file and getFCB/inode
OS allocates file descriptor from process's descriptor table
Creates entry in system-wide open file table
Copies relevant FCB information to open file table
File pointer initialized to beginning (or end for append)
Returns file descriptor to process
Subsequent read/write use this descriptor
Close() deallocates resources and updates tables
